Software 'Mori': Mori can resolve networking APIs from strings that are ADD-encrypted. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has utilized MDeployer to decrypt two payloads that contain MS4Killer toolkit b.cache and the Embargo ransomware executable a.cache with a hardcoded RC4 key `wlQYLoPCil3niI7x8CvR9EtNtL/aeaHrZ23LP3fAsJogVTIzdnZ5Pi09ZVeHFkiB`. Software 'Emotet': Emotet has used a self-extracting RAR file to deliver modules to victims. Emotet has also extracted embedded executables from files using hard-coded buffer offsets. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can use XOR 0x45 to decrypt obfuscated code. Software 'Green Lambert': Green Lambert can use multiple custom routines to decrypt strings prior to execution. Software 'Proton': Proton uses an encrypted file to store commands and configuration values. Software 'Gootloader': Gootloader has the ability to decode and decrypt malicious payloads prior to execution. Software 'INC Ransomware': INC Ransomware can run `CryptStringToBinaryA` to decrypt base64 content containing its ransom note. Software 'Kobalos': Kobalos decrypts strings right after the initial communication, but before the authentication process. Software 'BabyShark': BabyShark has the ability to decode downloaded files prior to execution. Software 'Carbon': Carbon decrypts task and configuration files for execution. Software 'HermeticWiper': HermeticWiper can decompress and copy driver files using `LZCopy`. Software 'Remexi': Remexi decrypts the configuration data using XOR with 25-character keys. Software 'gh0st RAT': gh0st RAT has decrypted and loaded the gh0st RAT DLL into memory, once the initial dropper executable is launched. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can deobfuscate Base64-encoded strings and scripts. Software 'OSX/Shlayer': OSX/Shlayer can base64-decode and AES-decrypt downloaded payloads. Versions of OSX/Shlayer pass encrypted and password-protected code to openssl and then write the payload to the /tmp folder. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has used Google Chrome's decryption and extraction operations. Software 'REPTILE': The REPTILE launcher component can decrypt kernel module code from a file and load it into memory. Software 'DropBook': DropBook can unarchive data downloaded from the C2 to obtain the payload and persistence modules. Software 'RegDuke': RegDuke can decrypt strings with a key either stored in the Registry or hardcoded in the code. Software 'Shamoon': Shamoon decrypts ciphertext using an XOR cipher and a base64-encoded string. Software 'DUSTPAN': DUSTPAN decodes and decrypts embedded payloads. Software 'HUI Loader': HUI Loader can decrypt and load files containing malicious payloads. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has the ability to download, unpack, and decrypt tar.gz files . Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT can decrypt strings using the victim's hostname as the key. Software 'Siloscape': Siloscape has decrypted the password of the C2 server with a simple byte by byte XOR. Siloscape also writes both an archive of Tor and the unzip binary to disk from data embedded within the payload using Visual Studio’s Resource Manager. Software 'MESSAGETAP': After checking for the existence of two files, keyword_parm.txt and parm.txt, MESSAGETAP XOR decodes and read the contents of the files. Software 'ShimRat': ShimRat has decompressed its core DLL using shellcode once an impersonated antivirus component was running on a system. Software 'Drovorub': Drovorub has de-obsfuscated XOR encrypted payloads in WebSocket messages. Software 'GoldMax': GoldMax has decoded and decrypted the configuration file when executed. Software 'MegaCortex': MegaCortex has used a Base64 key to decode its components. Software 'Lizar': Lizar has decrypted its configuration data, such as the C2 IP address, ports and other network communication. Software 'Turian': Turian has the ability to use a XOR decryption key to extract C2 server domains and IP addresses. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist can decode and decrypt messages received from C2. Software 'REvil': REvil can decode encrypted strings to enable execution of commands and payloads. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'THINCRUST': THINCRUST can deobfuscate RSA encrypted C2 commands received through the DEVICEID cookie. Software 'Agent Tesla': Agent Tesla has the ability to decrypt strings encrypted with the Rijndael symmetric encryption algorithm. Software 'MacMa': MacMa decrypts a downloaded file using AES-128-EBC with a custom delta. Software 'DEADEYE': DEADEYE has the ability to combine multiple sections of a binary which were broken up to evade detection into a single .dll prior to execution. Software 'WellMail': WellMail can decompress scripts received from C2. Software 'CoinTicker': CoinTicker decodes the initially-downloaded hidden encoded file using OpenSSL. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman has the ability to self-extract as a RAR archive. Software 'SharpStage': SharpStage has decompressed data received from the C2 server. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate installation includes binary code stored in a file located in a hidden directory, such as shell.txt, that is decrypted then executed. DarkGate uses hexadecimal-encoded shellcode payloads during installation that are called via Windows API CallWindowProc() to decode and then execute. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has decrypted the embedded HackBrowserData tool prior to execution. Software 'DEADWOOD': DEADWOOD XORs some strings within the binary using the value 0xD5, and deobfuscates these items at runtime. Software 'Sibot': Sibot can decrypt data received from a C2 and save to a file. Software 'SplatDropper': SplatDropper has decoded XOR encrypted payload. Software 'NativeZone': NativeZone can decrypt and decode embedded Cobalt Strike beacon stage shellcode. Software 'Pillowmint': Pillowmint has been decompressed by included shellcode prior to being launched. Software 'Hildegard': Hildegard has decrypted ELF files with AES. Software 'HTTPTroy': HTTPTroy has decoded strings encoded with Base64 and XOR prior to execution. Software 'IronWind': IronWind can deobfuscate the next stage payload using Base64 and XOR operations with the key "53". Software 'ComRAT': ComRAT has used unique per machine passwords to decrypt the orchestrator payload and a hardcoded XOR key to decrypt its communications module. ComRAT has also used a unique password to decrypt the file used for its hidden file system. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can decrypt its encrypted internal strings. Software 'Clambling': Clambling can deobfuscate its payload prior to execution. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot decodes the configuration data and modules. Software 'FIVEHANDS': FIVEHANDS has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Kapeka': Kapeka utilizes obfuscated JSON structures for various data storage and configuration management items. Software 'Saint Bot': Saint Bot can deobfuscate strings and files for execution. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can deobfuscate shellcode using a rolling XOR and decrypt metadata from Beacon sessions. The Cobalt Strike loader component can also decrypt the .bss section of the Beacon binary prior to execution. Software 'Shark': Shark can extract and decrypt downloaded .zip files. Software 'Spark': Spark has used a custom XOR algorithm to decrypt the payload. Software 'Okrum': Okrum's loader can decrypt the backdoor code, embedded within the loader or within a legitimate PNG file. A custom XOR cipher or RC4 is used for decryption. Software 'WindTail': WindTail has the ability to decrypt strings using hard-coded AES keys. Software 'XLoader': XLoader uses XOR and RC4 algorithms to decrypt payloads and functions. XLoader can be distributed as a self-extracting RAR archive that launches an AutoIT loader. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can decrypt, unpack and load a DLL from its resources, or from blobs encrypted with Data Protection API, two-key triple DES, and variations of the XOR cipher. Software 'HiddenWasp': HiddenWasp uses a cipher to implement a decoding function. Software 'ThreatNeedle': ThreatNeedle can decrypt its payload using RC4, AES, or one-byte XORing. Software 'SQLRat': SQLRat has scripts that are responsible for deobfuscating additional scripts. Software 'SUNSPOT': SUNSPOT decrypts SUNBURST, which was stored in AES128-CBC encrypted blobs. Software 'Valak': Valak has the ability to decode and decrypt downloaded files. Software 'TEARDROP': TEARDROP was decoded using a custom rolling XOR algorithm to execute a customized Cobalt Strike payload. Software 'PUNCHBUGGY': PUNCHBUGGY has used PowerShell to decode base64-encoded assembly. Software 'NOKKI': NOKKI uses a unique, custom de-obfuscation technique. Software 'Pikabot': Pikabot decrypts command and control URIs using ADVobfuscator, and decrypts IP addresses and port numbers with a custom algorithm. Other versions of Pikabot decode chunks of stored stage 2 payload content in the initial payload .text section before consolidating them for further execution. Overall LunarMail is associated with multiple encoding and encryption mechanisms to obfuscate the malware's presence and avoid analysis or detection. Software 'MagicRAT': MagicRAT stores command and control URLs using base64 encoding in the malware's configuration file. Software 'PS1': PS1 can use an XOR key to decrypt a PowerShell loader and payload binary. Software 'Cardinal RAT': Cardinal RAT decodes many of its artifacts and is decrypted (AES-128) after being downloaded. Software 'HexEval Loader': HexEval Loader has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'RustyWater': RustyWater has used the WriteHexToFile function to transform an embedded hex string to the payload CertificationKit.ini. Software 'PowerExchange': PowerExchange can decode and decrypt C2 commands received via email. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can deobfuscate downloaded files stored in reverse byte order and decrypt embedded resources using multiple XOR operations. Software 'GrimAgent': GrimAgent can use a decryption algorithm for strings based on Rotate on Right (RoR) and Rotate on Left (RoL) functionality. Software 'PowGoop': PowGoop can decrypt PowerShell scripts for execution. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer payloads have included strings encrypted via XOR. StrelaStealer JavaScript payloads utilize Base64-encoded payloads that are decoded via certutil to create a malicious DLL file. Software 'WellMess': WellMess can decode and decrypt data received from C2. Software 'FYAnti': FYAnti has the ability to decrypt an embedded .NET module. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin contains several layers of obfuscation to hide malicious code from detection and analysis. Software 'Goopy': Goopy has used a polymorphic decryptor to decrypt itself at runtime. Software 'WastedLocker': WastedLocker's custom cryptor, CryptOne, used an XOR based algorithm to decrypt the payload. Software 'PlugX': PlugX decompresses and decrypts itself using the Microsoft API call RtlDecompressBuffer. PlugX has also decrypted its payloads in memory. Software 'COATHANGER': COATHANGER decodes configuration items from a bundled file for command and control activity. Software 'Kerrdown': Kerrdown can decode, decrypt, and decompress multiple layers of shellcode. Software 'DanBot': DanBot can use a VBA macro to decode its payload prior to installation and execution. Software 'PingPull': PingPull can decrypt received data from its C2 server by using AES. Software 'BADFLICK': BADFLICK can decode shellcode using a custom rotating XOR cipher. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can decrypt command parameters sent through C2 and use unpacking code to extract its packed executable. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon can decrypt password-protected executables. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware is distributed as an obfuscated JavaScript launcher file. Software 'RGDoor': RGDoor decodes Base64 strings and decrypts strings using a custom XOR algorithm. Software 'FinFisher': FinFisher extracts and decrypts stage 3 malware, which is stored in encrypted resources. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has decoded antivirus name strings. Software 'STEADYPULSE': STEADYPULSE can URL decode key/value pairs sent over C2. Software 'AuditCred': AuditCred uses XOR and RC4 to perform decryption on the code functions. Software 'Winnti for Windows': The Winnti for Windows dropper can decrypt and decompresses a data blob. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can decrypt downloaded payloads. Bazar also resolves strings and other artifacts at runtime. Software 'Tsundere Botnet': Tsundere Botnet’s loader has decrypted obfuscated JavaScript files using the AES-256 CBC algorithm, a build-specific key, and initialization vector. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer uses RC4-encrypted, base64-encoded strings to obfuscate functionality and command and control servers. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT has used LZMA and base64 libraries to decode obfuscated scripts. Software 'PowerLess': PowerLess can use base64 and AES ECB decryption prior to execution of downloaded modules. Software 'OnionDuke': OnionDuke can use a custom decryption algorithm to decrypt strings. Software 'Zebrocy': Zebrocy decodes its secondary payload and writes it to the victim’s machine. Zebrocy also uses AES and XOR to decrypt strings and payloads. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda decrypts strings in the code during the execution process. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Spica': Upon execution Spica can decode an embedded .pdf and write it to the desktop as a decoy document. Software 'Fooder': Fooder has decrypted payloads using the WinCrypt API and the AES key. Software 'PITSTOP': PITSTOP can deobfuscate base64 encoded and AES encrypted commands. Software 'BackConfig': BackConfig has used a custom routine to decrypt strings. Software 'Cyclops Blink': Cyclops Blink can decrypt and parse instructions sent from C2. Software 'CLAIMLOADER': CLAIMLOADER has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has used AES and XOR to decrypt its DLLs. Software 'FRAMESTING': FRAMESTING can decompress data received within `POST` requests. Software 'Chaes': Chaes has decrypted an AES encrypted binary file to trigger the download of other files. Software 'Bandook': Bandook has decoded its PowerShell script. Software 'SoreFang': SoreFang can decode and decrypt exfiltrated data sent to C2. Software 'Ramsay': Ramsay can extract its agent from the body of a malicious document. Software 'MirageFox': MirageFox has a function for decrypting data containing C2 configuration information. Software 'xCaon': xCaon has decoded strings from the C2 server before executing commands. Software 'Metamorfo': Upon execution, Metamorfo has unzipped itself after being downloaded to the system and has performed string decryption. Software 'BUSHWALK': BUSHWALK can Base64 decode and RC4 decrypt malicious payloads sent through a web request’s command parameter. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY can decrypt encrypted strings and write them to a newly created folder. Software 'CostaBricks': CostaBricks has the ability to use bytecode to decrypt embedded payloads. Software 'RogueRobin': RogueRobin decodes an embedded executable using base64 and decompresses it. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer decrypts code to connect to a remote C2 server. Software 'StealBit': StealBit can deobfuscate loaded modules prior to execution. Software 'ODAgent': ODAgent can Base64-decode and XOR decrypt received C2 commands. Software 'OilBooster': OilBooster can Base64-decode and XOR-decrypt C2 commands taken from JSON files. Software 'ZxxZ': ZxxZ has used a XOR key to decrypt strings. Software 'Apostle': Apostle compiled code is obfuscated in an unspecified fashion prior to delivery to victims. Software 'Ecipekac': Ecipekac has the ability to decrypt fileless loader modules. Software 'FatDuke': FatDuke can decrypt AES encrypted C2 communications. Software 'Nightdoor': Nightdoor stores network configuration data in a file XOR encoded with the key value of `0x7A`. Software 'StarProxy': StarProxy has decrypted network packets using a custom algorithm. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer deobfuscates its strings and APIs once its executed. Software 'LookBack': LookBack has a function that decrypts malicious data. Software 'PolyglotDuke': PolyglotDuke can use a custom algorithm to decrypt strings used by the malware. Software 'Raindrop': Raindrop decrypted its Cobalt Strike payload using an AES-256 encryption algorithm in CBC mode with a unique key per sample. Software 'Dtrack': Dtrack has used a decryption routine that is part of an executable physical patch. Software 'Hancitor': Hancitor has decoded Base64 encoded URLs to insert a recipient’s name into the filename of the Word document. Hancitor has also extracted executables from ZIP files. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER downloads encoded payloads and decodes them on the victim. Group 'Turla': Turla has used a custom decryption routine, which pulls key and salt values from other artifacts such as a WMI filter or PowerShell Profile, to decode encrypted PowerShell payloads. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used malware to decrypt encrypted CAB files. Group 'WIRTE': WIRTE has used Base64 to decode malicious VBS script. Group 'Gorgon Group': Gorgon Group malware can decode contents from a payload that was Base64 encoded and write the contents to a file. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has decoded malicious VBScripts using Base64. Kimsuky has also decoded malicious PowerShell scripts using Base64. Kimsuky has decoded RC4 obfuscated files prior to downloading files from their infrastructure. Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet delivered payloads using multiple rounds of obfuscation and encoding to evade defenses and analysis. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Mustang Panda has also utilized RC4 encryption for malicious payloads. Group 'Agrius': Agrius has deployed base64-encoded variants of ASPXSpy to evade detection. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang has deobfuscated Base64-encoded shellcode strings prior to loading them. Group 'APT38': APT38 has used the RC4 algorithm to decrypt configuration data. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used Base64-encoded data to transfer payloads and commands, including deobfuscation via certutil. Group 'Molerats': Molerats decompresses ZIP files once on the victim machine. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has decrypted strings and imports using RC4 during execution. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used certutil to decode a string into a cabinet file. Group 'Storm-1811': Storm-1811 has distributed password-protected archives such as ZIP files during intrusions. Group 'ZIRCONIUM': ZIRCONIUM has used the AES256 algorithm with a SHA1 derived key to decrypt exploit code. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has decoded base64-encoded PowerShell, JavaScript, and VBScript. Group 'OilRig': A OilRig macro has run a PowerShell command to decode file contents. OilRig has also used certutil to decode base64-encoded files on victims.During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a script to concatenate and deobfuscate encoded strings in Mango. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group tools decrypted additional payloads from the C2. Gamaredon Group has also decoded Base64-encoded source code of a downloader. Additionally, Gamaredon Group has decoded Telegram content to reveal the IP address for C2 communications. Group 'TA505': TA505 has decrypted packed DLLs with an XOR key. Group 'Threat Group-3390': During execution, Threat Group-3390 malware deobfuscates and decompresses code that was encoded with Metasploit’s shikata_ga_nai encoder as well as compressed with LZNT1 compression. Group 'APT28': An APT28 macro uses the command certutil -decode to decode contents of a .txt file storing the base64 encoded payload.APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign was conducted by APT28 from early February 2022 to November 2024.During APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign, APT28 unarchived data using the GUI version of WinRAR. Group 'FIN7': FIN7 has decoded a malicious PowerShell script using `certutil -decode hex` and has decoded an XOR-obfuscated block of data with the key `qawsed1q2w3e`, which led to the installation of Lizar. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has used a script that decodes a Base64-encoded version of WeaveWorks Scope. Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern delivered exploit payloads via base64-encoded payloads in malicious email messages. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has used certutil in a macro to decode base64-encoded content contained in a dropper document attached to an email. The group has also used certutil -decode to decode files on the victim’s machine when dropping UPPERCUT. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group has used shellcode within macros to decrypt and manually map DLLs and shellcode into memory at runtime. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte has encoded commands in base64-encoded sections concatenated together in PowerShell. BlackByte uses PowerShell commands to disable Windows Defender. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan has used a DLL known as SeDll to decrypt and execute other JavaScript backdoors. Group 'Cinnamon Tempest': Cinnamon Tempest has used weaponized DLLs to load and decrypt payloads. Group 'Rocke': Rocke has extracted tar.gz files after downloading them from a C2 server. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper used shellcode with an XOR algorithm to decrypt a payload. Tropic Trooper also decrypted image files which contained a payload. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has utilized `certutil` to decode base64 encoded versions of custom malware. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team's VBS backdoor can decode Base64-encoded data and save it to the %TEMP% folder. The group also decrypted received information using the Triple DES algorithm and decompresses it using GZip. Group 'Malteiro': Malteiro has the ability to deobfuscate downloaded files prior to execution. Group 'APT19': An APT19 HTTP malware variant decrypts strings using single-byte XOR keys. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used certutil to decode Base64 binaries at runtime and a 16-byte XOR key to decrypt data. Group 'APT41': During C0017, APT41 used the DUSTPAN loader to decrypt embedded payloads. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used 7-Zip to decode their Raindrop malware. Group 'UNC3886': In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware implants to deobfuscate incoming C2 messages and encoded archives. Campaign 'Juicy Mix': During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a script to concatenate and deobfuscate encoded strings in Mango. Campaign 'Operation Spalax': For Operation Spalax, the threat actors used a variety of packers and droppers to decrypt malicious payloads. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors decrypted scripts prior to execution. Campaign 'C0017': During C0017, APT41 used the DUSTPAN loader to decrypt embedded payloads. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries decoded a Base64-encoded ZIP archive using the built-in certutil. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used 7-Zip to decode their Raindrop malware. Campaign 'APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign': During APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign, APT28 unarchived data using the GUI version of WinRAR. Campaign 'C0021': During C0021, the threat actors deobfuscated encoded PowerShell commands including use of the specific string `'FromBase'+0x40+'String'`, in place of `FromBase64String` which is normally used to decode base64. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware implants to deobfuscate incoming C2 messages and encoded archives. Campaign 'Water Curupira Pikabot Distribution': Water Curupira Pikabot Distribution used highly obfuscated JavaScript files as one initial installer for Pikabot. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors deobfuscated Base64-encoded commands following the execution of a malicious script, which revealed a small script designed to obtain an additional payload. Campaign 'Operation Dust Storm': During Operation Dust Storm, attackers used VBS code to decode payloads. Campaign 'Operation Honeybee': During Operation Honeybee, malicious files were decoded prior to execution. Campaign 'ArcaneDoor': ArcaneDoor involved the use of Base64 obfuscated scripts and commands.

CVE-2017-5638.title: Apache Struts Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
CVE-2017-5638.description: The Jakarta Multipart parser in Apache Struts 2 2.3.x before 2.3.32 and 2.5.x before 2.5.10.1 has incorrect exception handling and error-message generation during file-upload attempts, which allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands via a crafted Content-Type, Content-Disposition, or Content-Length HTTP header, as exploited in the wild in March 2017 with a Content-Type header containing a #cmd= string.
CVE-2017-5638.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2017-9805.title: Apache Struts Deserialization of Untrusted Data Vulnerability
CVE-2017-9805.description: The REST Plugin in Apache Struts 2.1.1 through 2.3.x before 2.3.34 and 2.5.x before 2.5.13 uses an XStreamHandler with an instance of XStream for deserialization without any type filtering, which can lead to Remote Code Execution when deserializing XML payloads.
CVE-2017-9805.versions: [{"product": "Apache Struts", "vendor": "Apache Software Foundation", "versions": [{"status": "affected", "version": "Apache Struts before 2.3.34 and 2.5.x before 2.5.13"}]}]
CVE-2017-9805.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2012-0391.title: Apache Struts 2 Improper Input Validation Vulnerability
CVE-2012-0391.description: The ExceptionDelegator component in Apache Struts before 2.2.3.1 interprets parameter values as OGNL expressions during certain exception handling for mismatched data types of properties, which allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary Java code via a crafted parameter.
CVE-2012-0391.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.
