T1140.name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
T1140.description: Adversaries may use [Obfuscated Files or Information](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027) to hide artifacts of an intrusion from analysis. They may require separate mechanisms to decode or deobfuscate that information depending on how they intend to use it. Methods for doing that include built-in functionality of malware or by using utilities present on the system. One such example is the use of [certutil](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0160) to decode a remote access tool portable executable file that has been hidden inside a certificate file.(Citation: Malwarebytes Targeted Attack against Saudi Arabia) Another example is using the Windows copy /b or type command to reassemble binary fragments into a malicious payload.(Citation: Carbon Black Obfuscation Sept 2016)(Citation: Sentinel One Tainted Love 2023) Sometimes a user's action may be required to open it for deobfuscation or decryption as part of [User Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204). The user may also be required to input a password to open a password protected compressed/encrypted file that was provided by the adversary.(Citation: Volexity PowerDuke November 2016)
T1140.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detect Adversary Deobfuscation or Decoding of Files and Payloads
- Analytic 'Analytic 0767': An adversary leverages built-in tools such as certutil.exe, powershell.exe, or copy.exe to decode, reassemble, or extract hidden malicious content from obfuscated containers or encoded formats. The decoding utility often spawns shortly after file staging or download and may be chained with script interpreters or further payload execution.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0768': The adversary uses native utilities like base64, gzip, tar, or openssl to decode, decompress, or decrypt files that were previously staged or downloaded. These tools may be chained with curl/wget and executed via bash/zsh, often to extract an embedded payload or reverse shell script.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0769': The adversary invokes built-in scripting or decoding tools like base64, plutil, or AppleScript-based utilities to decode files embedded in staging artifacts. Decoding often occurs post-download or as part of post-exploitation payload deployment via zsh, python, or osascript.
T1140.procedures: Software 'Imminent Monitor': Imminent Monitor has decoded malware components that are then dropped to the system. Software 'Expand': Expand can be used to decompress a local or remote CAB file into an executable. Software 'certutil': certutil has been used to decode binaries hidden inside certificate files as Base64 information. Software 'Brute Ratel C4': Brute Ratel C4 has the ability to deobfuscate its payload prior to execution. Software 'PcShare': PcShare has decrypted its strings by applying a XOR operation and a decompression using a custom implemented LZM algorithm. Software 'IronNetInjector': IronNetInjector has the ability to decrypt embedded .NET and PE payloads. Software 'PHPsert': PHPsert has the ability to decode and decrypt obfuscated strings prior to execution. Software 'SPAWNCHIMERA': SPAWNCHIMERA has decoded a XOR encoded private key. Software 'ZeroT': ZeroT shellcode decrypts and decompresses its RC4-encrypted payload. Software 'AppleJeus': AppleJeus has decoded files received from a C2. Software 'Action RAT': Action RAT can use Base64 to decode actor-controlled C2 server communications. Software 'KOCTOPUS': KOCTOPUS has deobfuscated itself before executing its commands. Software 'Snip3': Snip3 can decode its second-stage PowerShell script prior to execution. Software 'BendyBear': BendyBear has decrypted function blocks using a XOR key during runtime to evade detection. Software 'LiteDuke': LiteDuke has the ability to decrypt and decode multiple layers of obfuscation. Software 'P.A.S. Webshell': P.A.S. Webshell can use a decryption mechanism to process a user supplied password and allow execution. Software 'KONNI': KONNI has used certutil to download and decode base64 encoded strings and has also devoted a custom section to performing all the components of the deobfuscation process. Software 'Machete': Machete’s downloaded data is decrypted using AES. Software 'BOOSTWRITE': BOOSTWRITE has used a a 32-byte long multi-XOR key to decode data inside its payload. Software 'LockBit 3.0': The LockBit 3.0 payload is decrypted at runtime. Software 'RotaJakiro': RotaJakiro uses the AES algorithm, bit shifts in a function called `rotate`, and an XOR cipher to decrypt resources required for persistence, process guarding, and file locking. It also performs this same function on encrypted stack strings and the `head` and `key` sections in the network packet structure used for C2 communications. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs will decode malware components that are then dropped to the system. Software 'DarkTortilla': DarkTortilla can decrypt its payload and associated configuration elements using the Rijndael cipher. Software 'Gelsemium': Gelsemium can decompress and decrypt DLLs and shellcode. Software 'ROAMINGHOUSE': ROAMINGHOUSE can decode and drop a malicious ZIP file prior to execution. Software 'Smoke Loader': Smoke Loader deobfuscates its code. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu decrypts its encrypted configuration files prior to execution. Software 'Clop': Clop has used a simple XOR operation to decrypt strings. Software 'SDBbot': SDBbot has the ability to decrypt and decompress its payload to enable code execution. Software 'Babuk': Babuk has the ability to unpack itself into memory using XOR. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT uses Expand to decompress a CAB file into executable content. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can deobfuscate packed binaries in memory. Software 'Lokibot': Lokibot has decoded and decrypted its stages multiple times using hard-coded keys to deliver the final payload, and has decoded its server response hex string using XOR. Software 'POWERSTATS': POWERSTATS can deobfuscate the main backdoor code. Software 'QUADAGENT': QUADAGENT uses AES and a preshared key to decrypt the custom Base64 routine used to encode strings and scripts. Software 'VaporRage': VaporRage can deobfuscate XOR-encoded shellcode prior to execution. Software 'UPSTYLE': UPSTYLE encodes its main content prior to loading via Python as base64-encoded blobs. Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can decrypt its C2 address upon execution. Software 'Caminho': Caminho can deobfuscate downloaded files prior to execution. Software 'Avaddon': Avaddon has decrypted encrypted strings. Software 'Line Dancer': Line Dancer shellcode payloads are base64 encoded when transmitted to compromised devices. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT can run upload to decrypt and upload files from storage. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader can decode its payload prior to execution. Software 'Mongall': Mongall has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'WIREFIRE': WIREFIRE can decode, decrypt, and decompress data received in C2 HTTP `POST` requests. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb can be decrypted in memory using a Lightweight Encryption Algorithm (LEA)-128 key and decoded using a XOR key. Software 'PHASEJAM': PHASEJAM has the ability to decode Base64 commands and data. Software 'RAPIDPULSE': RAPIDPULSE listens for specific HTTP query parameters in received communications. If specific parameters match, a hard-coded RC4 key is used to decrypt the HTTP query paremter hmacTime. This decrypts to a filename that is then open, read, encrypted with the same RC4 key, base64-encoded, written to standard out, then passed as a response to the HTTP request. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has the ability to decrypt the loader configuration and payload DLL. Software 'ShadowPad': ShadowPad has decrypted a binary blob to start execution. Software 'QUIETCANARY': QUIETCANARY can use a custom parsing routine to decode the command codes and additional parameters from the C2 before executing them. Software 'Starloader': Starloader decrypts and executes shellcode from a file called Stars.jps. Software 'TSCookie': TSCookie has the ability to decrypt, load, and execute a DLL and its resources. Software 'Conti': Conti has decrypted its payload using a hardcoded AES-256 key. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs decrypts and extracts a copy of its main DLL payload when executing. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can decrypt its payload via a XOR key. Software 'DDKONG': DDKONG decodes an embedded configuration using XOR. Software 'Denis': Denis will decrypt important strings used for C&C communication. Software 'CASTLETAP': CASTLETAP can filter and deobfuscate an XOR encrypted activation string in the payload of an ICMP echo request. Software 'Bumblebee': Bumblebee can deobfuscate C2 server responses and unpack its code on targeted hosts. Software 'Final1stspy': Final1stspy uses Python code to deobfuscate base64-encoded strings. Software 'BoomBox': BoomBox can decrypt AES-encrypted files downloaded from C2. Software 'Chinoxy': The Chinoxy dropping function can initiate decryption of its config file. Software 'Azorult': Azorult uses an XOR key to decrypt content and uses Base64 to decode the C2 address. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth uses a fromCharCode() deobfuscation method to avoid explicitly writing execution commands and to hide its code. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can deobfuscate and re-assemble code strings for execution. Software 'LunarMail': LunarMail can decrypt strings to retrieve configuration settings. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet decrypts resources that are loaded into memory and executed. Software 'Netwalker': Netwalker's PowerShell script can decode and decrypt multiple layers of obfuscation, leading to the Netwalker DLL being loaded into memory. Software 'Lumma Stealer': Lumma Stealer has used Base64-encoded content during execution, decoded via PowerShell. Software 'GlassWorm': GlassWorm has decoded its Base64 instructions. GlassWorm has also decrypted its AES protected payloads. Software 'NOOPLDR': NOOPLDR can decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Squirrelwaffle': Squirrelwaffle has decrypted files and payloads using a XOR-based algorithm. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed can decode its payload prior to execution. Software 'LP-Notes': LP-Notes has decrypted strings with lengths ranging from 15 to 19 characters using the same decryption key for each string. Software 'ABK': ABK has the ability to decrypt AES encrypted payloads. Software 'BRUSHFIRE': BRUSHFIRE has decrypted XOR strings prior to execution. Software 'OopsIE': OopsIE concatenates then decompresses multiple resources to load an embedded .Net Framework assembly. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has decoded XOR-encrypted and Base-64-encoded payloads prior to execution. Software 'ANELLDR': ANELLDR can decrypt encrypted payload data using AES-256-CBC and subsequently execute the payload in memory. Software 'KEYPLUG': KEYPLUG can decode its configuration file to determine C2 protocols. Software 'SystemBC': SystemBC has the ability to decrypt RC4 encrypted packets and to decode obfuscated data before C2 communication. Additionally, SystemBC has decrypted its config file that was encoded with XOR and a hardcoded 40-byte key. Software 'LIGHTWIRE': LIGHTWIRE can RC4 decrypt and Base64 decode C2 commands. Software 'Winnti for Linux': Winnti for Linux has decoded XOR encoded strings holding its configuration upon execution. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can decrypt downloaded modules prior to execution. Software 'Ebury': Ebury has verified C2 domain ownership by decrypting the TXT record using an embedded RSA public key. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can decrypt files and data. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal has decoded strings in the malware using XOR and RC4. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D uses a decode routine combining bit shifting and XOR operations with a variable key that depends on the length of the string that was encoded. If the computation for the variable XOR key turns out to be 0, the default XOR key of 0x1B is used. This routine is also referenced as the `rotate` function in reporting. Software 'PyDCrypt': PyDCrypt has decrypted and dropped the DCSrv payload to disk. Software 'Rising Sun': Rising Sun has decrypted itself using a single-byte XOR scheme. Additionally, Rising Sun can decrypt its configuration data at runtime. Software 'Crimson': Crimson can decode its encoded PE file prior to execution. Software 'Torisma': Torisma has used XOR and Base64 to decode C2 data. Software 'CorKLOG': CorKLOG has decoded XOR encrypted strings. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor can decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Chrommme': Chrommme can decrypt its encrypted internal code. Software 'Bankshot': Bankshot decodes embedded XOR strings. Software 'VERMIN': VERMIN decrypts code, strings, and commands to use once it's on the victim's machine. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron has used AES and XOR to decrypt configuration files and commands. Software 'Kessel': Kessel has decrypted the binary's configuration once the main function was launched. Software 'GLASSTOKEN': GLASSTOKEN has the ability to decode hexadecimal and Base64 C2 requests. Software 'ISMInjector': ISMInjector uses the certutil command to decode a payload file. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic can first decrypt with the RC4 algorithm using a hardcoded decryption key before decompressing. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can decrypt and load other modules. Software 'Exbyte': Exbyte decodes and decrypts data stored in the configuration file with a key provided on the command line during execution. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus has the ability to deobfuscate encrypted strings. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor can use a stream cipher to decrypt stings used by the malware. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'CHIMNEYSWEEP': CHIMNEYSWEEP can use an embedded RC4 key to decrypt Windows API function strings. Software 'SampleCheck5000': SampleCheck5000 can decode and decrypt command line strings and files received through C2. Software 'HeartCrypt': HeartCrypt can decrypt payloads prior to execution. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb can decrypt strings related to communication configuration using RC4 with a static key. Software 'SLIGHTPULSE': SLIGHTPULSE can deobfuscate base64 encoded and RC4 encrypted C2 messages. Software 'RDAT': RDAT can deobfuscate the base64-encoded and AES-encrypted files downloaded from the C2 server. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer strings are deobfuscated prior to execution. Software 'Egregor': Egregor has been decrypted before execution. Software 'DOWNIISSA': DOWNIISSA can decode strings prior to execution. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub can use a provided passphrase to decrypt its configuration file. Software 'PureCrypter': PureCrypter can decrypt downloaded resources and parse internal files to determine its settings. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP deobfuscates embedded payloads. Software 'EnvyScout': EnvyScout can deobfuscate and write malicious ISO files to disk. Software 'Bundlore': Bundlore has used openssl to decrypt AES encrypted payload data. Bundlore has also used base64 and RC4 with a hardcoded key to deobfuscate data. Software 'RIFLESPINE': RIFLESPINE can deobfuscate encrypted files prior to execution on targeted hosts. Software 'MOPSLED': MOPSLED can decrypt obfuscated configuration files. Software 'HyperBro': HyperBro can unpack and decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Dyre': Dyre decrypts resources needed for targeting the victim. Software 'Waterbear': Waterbear has the ability to decrypt its RC4 encrypted payload for execution. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has used crypto key information stored in the Registry to decrypt Tor clients dropped to disk. Software 'Exaramel for Linux': Exaramel for Linux can decrypt its configuration file. Software 'ROADSWEEP': ROADSWEEP can decrypt embedded scripts prior to execution. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to decrypt files downloaded from C2. Software 'AvosLocker': AvosLocker has deobfuscated XOR-encoded strings. Software 'YAHOYAH': YAHOYAH decrypts downloaded files before execution. Software 'LockBit 2.0': LockBit 2.0 can decode scripts and strings in loaded modules. Software 'IceApple': IceApple can use a Base64-encoded AES key to decrypt tasking. Software 'BBK': BBK has the ability to decrypt AES encrypted payloads. Software 'TYPEFRAME': One TYPEFRAME variant decrypts an archive using an RC4 key, then decompresses and installs the decrypted malicious DLL module. Another variant decodes the embedded file by XORing it with the value "0x35". Software 'Ninja': The Ninja loader component can decrypt and decompress the payload. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has decoded XOR encrypted strings prior to execution in memory. Software 'Sagerunex': Sagerunex uses a custom decryption routine to unpack itself during installation. Software 'LunarLoader': LunarLoader can deobfuscate files containing the next stages in the infection chain. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has decoded its encrypted C2 traffic prior to execution. BRICKSTORM also has the ability to decode its obfuscated payload before execution. Software 'AshTag': The AshTag stager compoment can decode and decrypt Base64 and XOR-encrypted payloads. Software 'BOOKWORM': BOOKWORM has decoded its Base64 encoded payload prior to execution. BOOKWORM has also encrypted files with RC4 and has decrypted its payload prior to execution. Software 'Pteranodon': Pteranodon can decrypt encrypted data strings prior to using them. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can decode and drop a decoy file attached to spearphishing emails. Software 'Mori': Mori can resolve networking APIs from strings that are ADD-encrypted. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has utilized MDeployer to decrypt two payloads that contain MS4Killer toolkit b.cache and the Embargo ransomware executable a.cache with a hardcoded RC4 key `wlQYLoPCil3niI7x8CvR9EtNtL/aeaHrZ23LP3fAsJogVTIzdnZ5Pi09ZVeHFkiB`. Software 'Emotet': Emotet has used a self-extracting RAR file to deliver modules to victims. Emotet has also extracted embedded executables from files using hard-coded buffer offsets. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can use XOR 0x45 to decrypt obfuscated code. Software 'Green Lambert': Green Lambert can use multiple custom routines to decrypt strings prior to execution. Software 'Proton': Proton uses an encrypted file to store commands and configuration values. Software 'Gootloader': Gootloader has the ability to decode and decrypt malicious payloads prior to execution. Software 'INC Ransomware': INC Ransomware can run `CryptStringToBinaryA` to decrypt base64 content containing its ransom note. Software 'Kobalos': Kobalos decrypts strings right after the initial communication, but before the authentication process. Software 'BabyShark': BabyShark has the ability to decode downloaded files prior to execution. Software 'Carbon': Carbon decrypts task and configuration files for execution. Software 'HermeticWiper': HermeticWiper can decompress and copy driver files using `LZCopy`. Software 'Remexi': Remexi decrypts the configuration data using XOR with 25-character keys. Software 'gh0st RAT': gh0st RAT has decrypted and loaded the gh0st RAT DLL into memory, once the initial dropper executable is launched. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can deobfuscate Base64-encoded strings and scripts. Software 'OSX/Shlayer': OSX/Shlayer can base64-decode and AES-decrypt downloaded payloads. Versions of OSX/Shlayer pass encrypted and password-protected code to openssl and then write the payload to the /tmp folder. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has used Google Chrome's decryption and extraction operations. Software 'REPTILE': The REPTILE launcher component can decrypt kernel module code from a file and load it into memory. Software 'DropBook': DropBook can unarchive data downloaded from the C2 to obtain the payload and persistence modules. Software 'RegDuke': RegDuke can decrypt strings with a key either stored in the Registry or hardcoded in the code. Software 'Shamoon': Shamoon decrypts ciphertext using an XOR cipher and a base64-encoded string. Software 'DUSTPAN': DUSTPAN decodes and decrypts embedded payloads. Software 'HUI Loader': HUI Loader can decrypt and load files containing malicious payloads. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has the ability to download, unpack, and decrypt tar.gz files . Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT can decrypt strings using the victim's hostname as the key. Software 'Siloscape': Siloscape has decrypted the password of the C2 server with a simple byte by byte XOR. Siloscape also writes both an archive of Tor and the unzip binary to disk from data embedded within the payload using Visual Studio’s Resource Manager. Software 'MESSAGETAP': After checking for the existence of two files, keyword_parm.txt and parm.txt, MESSAGETAP XOR decodes and read the contents of the files. Software 'ShimRat': ShimRat has decompressed its core DLL using shellcode once an impersonated antivirus component was running on a system. Software 'Drovorub': Drovorub has de-obsfuscated XOR encrypted payloads in WebSocket messages. Software 'GoldMax': GoldMax has decoded and decrypted the configuration file when executed. Software 'MegaCortex': MegaCortex has used a Base64 key to decode its components. Software 'Lizar': Lizar has decrypted its configuration data, such as the C2 IP address, ports and other network communication. Software 'Turian': Turian has the ability to use a XOR decryption key to extract C2 server domains and IP addresses. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist can decode and decrypt messages received from C2. Software 'REvil': REvil can decode encrypted strings to enable execution of commands and payloads. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'THINCRUST': THINCRUST can deobfuscate RSA encrypted C2 commands received through the DEVICEID cookie. Software 'Agent Tesla': Agent Tesla has the ability to decrypt strings encrypted with the Rijndael symmetric encryption algorithm. Software 'MacMa': MacMa decrypts a downloaded file using AES-128-EBC with a custom delta. Software 'DEADEYE': DEADEYE has the ability to combine multiple sections of a binary which were broken up to evade detection into a single .dll prior to execution. Software 'WellMail': WellMail can decompress scripts received from C2. Software 'CoinTicker': CoinTicker decodes the initially-downloaded hidden encoded file using OpenSSL. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman has the ability to self-extract as a RAR archive. Software 'SharpStage': SharpStage has decompressed data received from the C2 server. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate installation includes binary code stored in a file located in a hidden directory, such as shell.txt, that is decrypted then executed. DarkGate uses hexadecimal-encoded shellcode payloads during installation that are called via Windows API CallWindowProc() to decode and then execute. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has decrypted the embedded HackBrowserData tool prior to execution. Software 'DEADWOOD': DEADWOOD XORs some strings within the binary using the value 0xD5, and deobfuscates these items at runtime. Software 'Sibot': Sibot can decrypt data received from a C2 and save to a file. Software 'SplatDropper': SplatDropper has decoded XOR encrypted payload. Software 'NativeZone': NativeZone can decrypt and decode embedded Cobalt Strike beacon stage shellcode. Software 'Pillowmint': Pillowmint has been decompressed by included shellcode prior to being launched. Software 'Hildegard': Hildegard has decrypted ELF files with AES. Software 'HTTPTroy': HTTPTroy has decoded strings encoded with Base64 and XOR prior to execution. Software 'IronWind': IronWind can deobfuscate the next stage payload using Base64 and XOR operations with the key "53". Software 'ComRAT': ComRAT has used unique per machine passwords to decrypt the orchestrator payload and a hardcoded XOR key to decrypt its communications module. ComRAT has also used a unique password to decrypt the file used for its hidden file system. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can decrypt its encrypted internal strings. Software 'Clambling': Clambling can deobfuscate its payload prior to execution. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot decodes the configuration data and modules. Software 'FIVEHANDS': FIVEHANDS has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Kapeka': Kapeka utilizes obfuscated JSON structures for various data storage and configuration management items. Software 'Saint Bot': Saint Bot can deobfuscate strings and files for execution. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can deobfuscate shellcode using a rolling XOR and decrypt metadata from Beacon sessions. The Cobalt Strike loader component can also decrypt the .bss section of the Beacon binary prior to execution. Software 'Shark': Shark can extract and decrypt downloaded .zip files. Software 'Spark': Spark has used a custom XOR algorithm to decrypt the payload. Software 'Okrum': Okrum's loader can decrypt the backdoor code, embedded within the loader or within a legitimate PNG file. A custom XOR cipher or RC4 is used for decryption. Software 'WindTail': WindTail has the ability to decrypt strings using hard-coded AES keys. Software 'XLoader': XLoader uses XOR and RC4 algorithms to decrypt payloads and functions. XLoader can be distributed as a self-extracting RAR archive that launches an AutoIT loader. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can decrypt, unpack and load a DLL from its resources, or from blobs encrypted with Data Protection API, two-key triple DES, and variations of the XOR cipher. Software 'HiddenWasp': HiddenWasp uses a cipher to implement a decoding function. Software 'ThreatNeedle': ThreatNeedle can decrypt its payload using RC4, AES, or one-byte XORing. Software 'SQLRat': SQLRat has scripts that are responsible for deobfuscating additional scripts. Software 'SUNSPOT': SUNSPOT decrypts SUNBURST, which was stored in AES128-CBC encrypted blobs. Software 'Valak': Valak has the ability to decode and decrypt downloaded files. Software 'TEARDROP': TEARDROP was decoded using a custom rolling XOR algorithm to execute a customized Cobalt Strike payload. Software 'PUNCHBUGGY': PUNCHBUGGY has used PowerShell to decode base64-encoded assembly. Software 'NOKKI': NOKKI uses a unique, custom de-obfuscation technique. Software 'Pikabot': Pikabot decrypts command and control URIs using ADVobfuscator, and decrypts IP addresses and port numbers with a custom algorithm. Other versions of Pikabot decode chunks of stored stage 2 payload content in the initial payload .text section before consolidating them for further execution. Overall LunarMail is associated with multiple encoding and encryption mechanisms to obfuscate the malware's presence and avoid analysis or detection. Software 'MagicRAT': MagicRAT stores command and control URLs using base64 encoding in the malware's configuration file. Software 'PS1': PS1 can use an XOR key to decrypt a PowerShell loader and payload binary. Software 'Cardinal RAT': Cardinal RAT decodes many of its artifacts and is decrypted (AES-128) after being downloaded. Software 'HexEval Loader': HexEval Loader has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'RustyWater': RustyWater has used the WriteHexToFile function to transform an embedded hex string to the payload CertificationKit.ini. Software 'PowerExchange': PowerExchange can decode and decrypt C2 commands received via email. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can deobfuscate downloaded files stored in reverse byte order and decrypt embedded resources using multiple XOR operations. Software 'GrimAgent': GrimAgent can use a decryption algorithm for strings based on Rotate on Right (RoR) and Rotate on Left (RoL) functionality. Software 'PowGoop': PowGoop can decrypt PowerShell scripts for execution. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer payloads have included strings encrypted via XOR. StrelaStealer JavaScript payloads utilize Base64-encoded payloads that are decoded via certutil to create a malicious DLL file. Software 'WellMess': WellMess can decode and decrypt data received from C2. Software 'FYAnti': FYAnti has the ability to decrypt an embedded .NET module. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin contains several layers of obfuscation to hide malicious code from detection and analysis. Software 'Goopy': Goopy has used a polymorphic decryptor to decrypt itself at runtime. Software 'WastedLocker': WastedLocker's custom cryptor, CryptOne, used an XOR based algorithm to decrypt the payload. Software 'PlugX': PlugX decompresses and decrypts itself using the Microsoft API call RtlDecompressBuffer. PlugX has also decrypted its payloads in memory. Software 'COATHANGER': COATHANGER decodes configuration items from a bundled file for command and control activity. Software 'Kerrdown': Kerrdown can decode, decrypt, and decompress multiple layers of shellcode. Software 'DanBot': DanBot can use a VBA macro to decode its payload prior to installation and execution. Software 'PingPull': PingPull can decrypt received data from its C2 server by using AES. Software 'BADFLICK': BADFLICK can decode shellcode using a custom rotating XOR cipher. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can decrypt command parameters sent through C2 and use unpacking code to extract its packed executable. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon can decrypt password-protected executables. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware is distributed as an obfuscated JavaScript launcher file. Software 'RGDoor': RGDoor decodes Base64 strings and decrypts strings using a custom XOR algorithm. Software 'FinFisher': FinFisher extracts and decrypts stage 3 malware, which is stored in encrypted resources. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has decoded antivirus name strings. Software 'STEADYPULSE': STEADYPULSE can URL decode key/value pairs sent over C2. Software 'AuditCred': AuditCred uses XOR and RC4 to perform decryption on the code functions. Software 'Winnti for Windows': The Winnti for Windows dropper can decrypt and decompresses a data blob. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can decrypt downloaded payloads. Bazar also resolves strings and other artifacts at runtime. Software 'Tsundere Botnet': Tsundere Botnet’s loader has decrypted obfuscated JavaScript files using the AES-256 CBC algorithm, a build-specific key, and initialization vector. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer uses RC4-encrypted, base64-encoded strings to obfuscate functionality and command and control servers. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT has used LZMA and base64 libraries to decode obfuscated scripts. Software 'PowerLess': PowerLess can use base64 and AES ECB decryption prior to execution of downloaded modules. Software 'OnionDuke': OnionDuke can use a custom decryption algorithm to decrypt strings. Software 'Zebrocy': Zebrocy decodes its secondary payload and writes it to the victim’s machine. Zebrocy also uses AES and XOR to decrypt strings and payloads. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda decrypts strings in the code during the execution process. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Software 'Spica': Upon execution Spica can decode an embedded .pdf and write it to the desktop as a decoy document. Software 'Fooder': Fooder has decrypted payloads using the WinCrypt API and the AES key. Software 'PITSTOP': PITSTOP can deobfuscate base64 encoded and AES encrypted commands. Software 'BackConfig': BackConfig has used a custom routine to decrypt strings. Software 'Cyclops Blink': Cyclops Blink can decrypt and parse instructions sent from C2. Software 'CLAIMLOADER': CLAIMLOADER has decoded its payload prior to execution. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has used AES and XOR to decrypt its DLLs. Software 'FRAMESTING': FRAMESTING can decompress data received within `POST` requests. Software 'Chaes': Chaes has decrypted an AES encrypted binary file to trigger the download of other files. Software 'Bandook': Bandook has decoded its PowerShell script. Software 'SoreFang': SoreFang can decode and decrypt exfiltrated data sent to C2. Software 'Ramsay': Ramsay can extract its agent from the body of a malicious document. Software 'MirageFox': MirageFox has a function for decrypting data containing C2 configuration information. Software 'xCaon': xCaon has decoded strings from the C2 server before executing commands. Software 'Metamorfo': Upon execution, Metamorfo has unzipped itself after being downloaded to the system and has performed string decryption. Software 'BUSHWALK': BUSHWALK can Base64 decode and RC4 decrypt malicious payloads sent through a web request’s command parameter. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY can decrypt encrypted strings and write them to a newly created folder. Software 'CostaBricks': CostaBricks has the ability to use bytecode to decrypt embedded payloads. Software 'RogueRobin': RogueRobin decodes an embedded executable using base64 and decompresses it. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer decrypts code to connect to a remote C2 server. Software 'StealBit': StealBit can deobfuscate loaded modules prior to execution. Software 'ODAgent': ODAgent can Base64-decode and XOR decrypt received C2 commands. Software 'OilBooster': OilBooster can Base64-decode and XOR-decrypt C2 commands taken from JSON files. Software 'ZxxZ': ZxxZ has used a XOR key to decrypt strings. Software 'Apostle': Apostle compiled code is obfuscated in an unspecified fashion prior to delivery to victims. Software 'Ecipekac': Ecipekac has the ability to decrypt fileless loader modules. Software 'FatDuke': FatDuke can decrypt AES encrypted C2 communications. Software 'Nightdoor': Nightdoor stores network configuration data in a file XOR encoded with the key value of `0x7A`. Software 'StarProxy': StarProxy has decrypted network packets using a custom algorithm. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer deobfuscates its strings and APIs once its executed. Software 'LookBack': LookBack has a function that decrypts malicious data. Software 'PolyglotDuke': PolyglotDuke can use a custom algorithm to decrypt strings used by the malware. Software 'Raindrop': Raindrop decrypted its Cobalt Strike payload using an AES-256 encryption algorithm in CBC mode with a unique key per sample. Software 'Dtrack': Dtrack has used a decryption routine that is part of an executable physical patch. Software 'Hancitor': Hancitor has decoded Base64 encoded URLs to insert a recipient’s name into the filename of the Word document. Hancitor has also extracted executables from ZIP files. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER downloads encoded payloads and decodes them on the victim. Group 'Turla': Turla has used a custom decryption routine, which pulls key and salt values from other artifacts such as a WMI filter or PowerShell Profile, to decode encrypted PowerShell payloads. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used malware to decrypt encrypted CAB files. Group 'WIRTE': WIRTE has used Base64 to decode malicious VBS script. Group 'Gorgon Group': Gorgon Group malware can decode contents from a payload that was Base64 encoded and write the contents to a file. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has decoded malicious VBScripts using Base64. Kimsuky has also decoded malicious PowerShell scripts using Base64. Kimsuky has decoded RC4 obfuscated files prior to downloading files from their infrastructure. Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet delivered payloads using multiple rounds of obfuscation and encoding to evade defenses and analysis. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has the ability to decrypt its payload prior to execution. Mustang Panda has also utilized RC4 encryption for malicious payloads. Group 'Agrius': Agrius has deployed base64-encoded variants of ASPXSpy to evade detection. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang has deobfuscated Base64-encoded shellcode strings prior to loading them. Group 'APT38': APT38 has used the RC4 algorithm to decrypt configuration data. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used Base64-encoded data to transfer payloads and commands, including deobfuscation via certutil. Group 'Molerats': Molerats decompresses ZIP files once on the victim machine. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has decrypted strings and imports using RC4 during execution. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used certutil to decode a string into a cabinet file. Group 'Storm-1811': Storm-1811 has distributed password-protected archives such as ZIP files during intrusions. Group 'ZIRCONIUM': ZIRCONIUM has used the AES256 algorithm with a SHA1 derived key to decrypt exploit code. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has decoded base64-encoded PowerShell, JavaScript, and VBScript. Group 'OilRig': A OilRig macro has run a PowerShell command to decode file contents. OilRig has also used certutil to decode base64-encoded files on victims.During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a script to concatenate and deobfuscate encoded strings in Mango. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group tools decrypted additional payloads from the C2. Gamaredon Group has also decoded Base64-encoded source code of a downloader. Additionally, Gamaredon Group has decoded Telegram content to reveal the IP address for C2 communications. Group 'TA505': TA505 has decrypted packed DLLs with an XOR key. Group 'Threat Group-3390': During execution, Threat Group-3390 malware deobfuscates and decompresses code that was encoded with Metasploit’s shikata_ga_nai encoder as well as compressed with LZNT1 compression. Group 'APT28': An APT28 macro uses the command certutil -decode to decode contents of a .txt file storing the base64 encoded payload.APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign was conducted by APT28 from early February 2022 to November 2024.During APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign, APT28 unarchived data using the GUI version of WinRAR. Group 'FIN7': FIN7 has decoded a malicious PowerShell script using `certutil -decode hex` and has decoded an XOR-obfuscated block of data with the key `qawsed1q2w3e`, which led to the installation of Lizar. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has used a script that decodes a Base64-encoded version of WeaveWorks Scope. Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern delivered exploit payloads via base64-encoded payloads in malicious email messages. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has used certutil in a macro to decode base64-encoded content contained in a dropper document attached to an email. The group has also used certutil -decode to decode files on the victim’s machine when dropping UPPERCUT. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group has used shellcode within macros to decrypt and manually map DLLs and shellcode into memory at runtime. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte has encoded commands in base64-encoded sections concatenated together in PowerShell. BlackByte uses PowerShell commands to disable Windows Defender. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan has used a DLL known as SeDll to decrypt and execute other JavaScript backdoors. Group 'Cinnamon Tempest': Cinnamon Tempest has used weaponized DLLs to load and decrypt payloads. Group 'Rocke': Rocke has extracted tar.gz files after downloading them from a C2 server. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper used shellcode with an XOR algorithm to decrypt a payload. Tropic Trooper also decrypted image files which contained a payload. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has utilized `certutil` to decode base64 encoded versions of custom malware. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team's VBS backdoor can decode Base64-encoded data and save it to the %TEMP% folder. The group also decrypted received information using the Triple DES algorithm and decompresses it using GZip. Group 'Malteiro': Malteiro has the ability to deobfuscate downloaded files prior to execution. Group 'APT19': An APT19 HTTP malware variant decrypts strings using single-byte XOR keys. Group 'Higaisa': Higaisa used certutil to decode Base64 binaries at runtime and a 16-byte XOR key to decrypt data. Group 'APT41': During C0017, APT41 used the DUSTPAN loader to decrypt embedded payloads. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used 7-Zip to decode their Raindrop malware. Group 'UNC3886': In mid-2024 Mandiant identified custom TINYSHELL-based backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks’ Junos OS routers. Mandiant attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group UNC3886.During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware implants to deobfuscate incoming C2 messages and encoded archives. Campaign 'Juicy Mix': During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a script to concatenate and deobfuscate encoded strings in Mango. Campaign 'Operation Spalax': For Operation Spalax, the threat actors used a variety of packers and droppers to decrypt malicious payloads. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors decrypted scripts prior to execution. Campaign 'C0017': During C0017, APT41 used the DUSTPAN loader to decrypt embedded payloads. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries decoded a Base64-encoded ZIP archive using the built-in certutil. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used 7-Zip to decode their Raindrop malware. Campaign 'APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign': During APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign, APT28 unarchived data using the GUI version of WinRAR. Campaign 'C0021': During C0021, the threat actors deobfuscated encoded PowerShell commands including use of the specific string `'FromBase'+0x40+'String'`, in place of `FromBase64String` which is normally used to decode base64. Campaign 'RedPenguin': During RedPenguin, UNC3886 used malware implants to deobfuscate incoming C2 messages and encoded archives. Campaign 'Water Curupira Pikabot Distribution': Water Curupira Pikabot Distribution used highly obfuscated JavaScript files as one initial installer for Pikabot. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors deobfuscated Base64-encoded commands following the execution of a malicious script, which revealed a small script designed to obtain an additional payload. Campaign 'Operation Dust Storm': During Operation Dust Storm, attackers used VBS code to decode payloads. Campaign 'Operation Honeybee': During Operation Honeybee, malicious files were decoded prior to execution. Campaign 'ArcaneDoor': ArcaneDoor involved the use of Base64 obfuscated scripts and commands.

T1027.013.name: Encrypted/Encoded File
T1027.013.description:
Adversaries may encrypt or encode files to obfuscate strings, bytes, and other specific patterns to impede detection. Encrypting and/or encoding file content aims to conceal malicious artifacts within a file used in an intrusion. Many other techniques, such as [Software Packing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027/002), [Steganography](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027/003), and [Embedded Payloads](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1027/009), share this same broad objective. Encrypting and/or encoding files could lead to a lapse in detection of static signatures, only for this malicious content to be revealed (i.e., [Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1140)) at the time of execution/use.

This type of file obfuscation can be applied to many file artifacts present on victim hosts, such as malware log/configuration and payload files.(Citation: File obfuscation) Files can be encrypted with a hardcoded or user-supplied key, as well as otherwise obfuscated using standard encoding schemes such as Base64.

The entire content of a file may be obfuscated, or just specific functions or values (such as C2 addresses). Encryption and encoding may also be applied in redundant layers for additional protection.

For example, adversaries may abuse password-protected Word documents or self-extracting (SFX) archives as a method of encrypting/encoding a file such as a [Phishing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566) payload. These files typically function by attaching the intended archived content to a decompressor stub that is executed when the file is invoked (e.g., [User Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204)).(Citation: SFX - Encrypted/Encoded File) 

Adversaries may also abuse file-specific as well as custom encoding schemes. For example, Byte Order Mark (BOM) headers in text files may be abused to manipulate and obfuscate file content until [Command and Scripting Interpreter](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059) execution.
T1027.013.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Antivirus/Antimalware': Anti-virus can be used to automatically detect and quarantine suspicious files, including those with high entropy measurements or with otherwise potentially malicious signs of obfuscation.
Mitigation 'Behavior Prevention on Endpoint': On Windows 10+, enable Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules to block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts. Security tools should be configured to analyze the encoding properties of files and detect anomalies that deviate from standard encoding practices.
T1027.013.detections:
Detection Strategy: Encrypted or Encoded File Payload Detection Strategy
- Analytic 'Analytic 0237': Detection of processes that load or decode encrypted/encoded files in memory and subsequently execute or inject them, indicating payload unpacking or memory-resident malware.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0238': Detection of suspicious use of shell utilities or scripts that decode or decrypt a payload and execute it without writing to disk.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0239': Detection of encoded payloads being decoded and executed in-memory using scripting tools or third-party decoders.
T1027.013.procedures: Software 'Donut': Donut can generate encrypted, compressed/encoded, or otherwise obfuscated code modules. Software 'PcShare': PcShare has been encrypted with XOR using different 32-long Base16 strings. Software 'Sliver': Sliver can encrypt strings at compile time. Software 'Remcos': Remcos can use string encryption to hinder analysis. Software 'DCRAT': The DCRAT configuration file is encrypted using AES-256. Software 'IronNetInjector': IronNetInjector can obfuscate variable names, encrypt strings, as well as base64 encode and Rijndael encrypt payloads. Software 'DEADEYE': DEADEYE has encrypted its payload. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can employ several code obfuscation methods, including renaming functions, altering control flows, and encrypting strings. Software 'Torisma': Torisma has been Base64 encoded and AES encrypted. Software 'OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D': OSX_OCEANLOTUS.D encrypts its strings in RSA256 and encodes them in a custom base64 scheme and XOR. Software 'USBStealer': Most strings in USBStealer are encrypted using 3DES and XOR and reversed. Software 'Emissary': Variants of Emissary encrypt payloads using various XOR ciphers, as well as a custom algorithm that uses the "srand" and "rand" functions. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer strings are XOR-encrypted. Software 'Kessel': Kessel's configuration is hardcoded and RC4 encrypted within the binary. Software 'Raindrop': Raindrop encrypted its payload using a simple XOR algorithm with a single-byte key. Software 'Rifdoor': Rifdoor has encrypted strings with a single byte XOR algorithm. Software 'Shark': Shark can use encrypted and encoded files for C2 configuration. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has used an XOR-based algorithm to encrypt Tor clients dropped to disk. Ursnif droppers have also been delivered as password-protected zip files that execute base64 encoded PowerShell commands. Software 'RustyWater': RustyWater has encrypted all strings in the code using position independent XOR encryption. Software 'ROADSWEEP': The ROADSWEEP binary contains RC4 encrypted embedded scripts. Software 'PAKLOG': PAKLOG has utilized a simple encoding mechanism to encode characters in the buffer. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub has an encrypted configuration file. Software 'Ninja': The Ninja payload is XOR encrypted and compressed. Ninja has also XORed its configuration data with a constant value of `0xAA`. Software 'HAWKBALL': HAWKBALL has encrypted the payload with an XOR-based algorithm. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has encrypted it's main payload using 3DES. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth has used an XOR-based algorithm to encrypt payloads twice with different keys. Software 'KEYPLUG': KEYPLUG can use a hardcoded one-byte XOR encoded configuration file. Software 'Sakula': Sakula uses single-byte XOR obfuscation to obfuscate many of its files. Software 'Zox': Zox has been encoded with Base64. Software 'Helminth': The Helminth config file is encrypted with RC4. Software 'SamSam': SamSam has been seen using AES or DES to encrypt payloads and payload components. Software 'Prikormka': Some resources in Prikormka are encrypted with a simple XOR operation or encoded with Base64. Software 'NanHaiShu': NanHaiShu encodes files in Base64. Software 'metaMain': metaMain's module file has been encrypted via XOR. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has utilized XOR encrypted strings. Software 'Bazar': Bazar has used XOR, RSA2, and RC4 encrypted files. Software 'LockBit 3.0': The LockBit 3.0 payload includes an encrypted main component. Software 'LoudMiner': LoudMiner has encrypted DMG files. Software 'DOWNIISSA': DOWNIISSA code is base64 encoded and XOR encrypted. Software 'MEDUSA': MEDUSA can XOR encrypt configuration strings. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT has a XOR-encoded payload. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon modules are stored encrypted on disk. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT has used Base64 encoded strings and scripts. Software 'DCSrv': DCSrv's configuration is encrypted. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace has encrypted its payload with AES. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT supports file encryption (AES with the key "lolomycin2017"). Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has encrypted payloads and strings. Software 'XLoader': XLoader features encrypted functions using the RC4 algorithm and bytecode operations. Software 'Sagerunex': Sagerunex can be passed a reference to an XOR-encrypted configuration file at runtime. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer uses XOR-encoded strings to obfuscate items. Software 'LightNeuron': LightNeuron encrypts its configuration files with AES-256. Software 'DanBot': DanBot can Base64 encode its payload. Software 'FELIXROOT': FELIXROOT encrypts strings in the backdoor using a custom XOR algorithm. Software 'Remsec': Some data in Remsec is encrypted using RC5 in CBC mode, AES-CBC with a hardcoded key, RC4, or Salsa20. Some data is also base64-encoded. Software 'SocGholish': SocGholish has single or double Base-64 encoded references to its second-stage server URLs. Software 'NOOPLDR': The NOOPLDR payload is encrypted with AES256-CBC. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP begins with an initial launcher that decrypts an AES-128-CFB encrypted file on disk and executes it in memory. Software 'MOPSLED': MOPSLED can encrypt configuration files with a custom ChaCha20 algorithm. Software 'WindTail': WindTail can be delivered as a compressed, encrypted, and encoded payload. Software 'Grandoreiro': The Grandoreiro payload has been delivered encrypted with a custom XOR-based algorithm and also as a base64-encoded ZIP file. Software 'Milan': Milan can encode files containing information about the targeted system. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN has obfuscated code using Base64 encoding. Software 'KONNI': KONNI is heavily obfuscated and includes encrypted configuration files. Software 'TINYTYPHON': TINYTYPHON has used XOR with 0x90 to obfuscate its configuration file. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can Base64 encode strings, store downloaded files in reverse byte order, and use the Eazfuscator tool to obfuscate its third stage. Software 'ZxxZ': ZxxZ has been encoded to avoid detection from static analysis tools. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate drops an encrypted PE file, pe.bin, and decrypts it during installation. DarkGate also uses custom base64 encoding schemas in later variations to obfuscate payloads. Software 'Reaver': Reaver encrypts some of its files with XOR. Software 'BOOSTWRITE': BOOSTWRITE has encoded its payloads using a ChaCha stream cipher with a 256-bit key and 64-bit Initialization vector (IV) to evade detection. Software 'Fysbis': Fysbis has been encrypted using XOR and RC4. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet uses encrypted configuration blocks and writes encrypted files to disk. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer has used RC4-encrypted strings and string hashes to avoid identifiable strings within the binary. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs downloads additional files that are base64-encoded and encrypted with another cipher. Software 'Remexi': Remexi obfuscates its configuration data with XOR. Software 'TYPEFRAME': APIs and strings in some TYPEFRAME variants are RC4 encrypted. Another variant is encoded with XOR. Software 'Mango': Mango contains a series of base64 encoded substrings. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb has been XOR-encoded. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity has used encrypted strings in its dropper component. Software 'GlassWorm': GlassWorm has leveraged AES-256-CBC encryption to obfuscate its malicious JavaScript payload. GlassWorm has also utilized Base64 encoding to obfuscate the C2 details stored in the Solana memo field. Software 'LightSpy': LightSpy encrypts the C2 configuration file using AES with a static key, while the module `.dylib` files use a rolling one-byte encoding for obfuscation. Software 'MagicRAT': MagicRAT stores base64 encoded command and contorl URLs in a configuraiton file, with each URL prefixed with the value `LR02DPt22R`. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has utilized XOR cipher encryption to hide key strings within their code, to include IPv4 addresses of public DNS-over-HTTPS (DOH) servers. Software 'VersaMem': VersaMem encrypted captured credentials with AES then Base64 encoded them before writing to local storage. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal's DLL file and non-malicious decoy file are encrypted with RC4 and some function name strings are obfuscated. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer uses RC4 encryption for strings and command and control addresses to evade static detection. Software 'LODEINFO': The LODEINFO loader module contains XOR-encrypted shellcode. Software 'RedLeaves': A RedLeaves configuration file is encrypted with a simple XOR key, 0x53. Software 'PHASEJAM': PHASEJAM has launched a webshell using the `MIME::Base64` module that encoded and decoded Base64 commands. Software 'AshTag': The AshTag AshenOrchestrator component payload as been Base64 encoded and embedded with HTML content from the C2 server. Software 'UPSTYLE': UPSTYLE stores primary content as base64-encoded objects. Software 'FlawedGrace': FlawedGrace encrypts its C2 configuration files with AES in CBC mode. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs's payload has been encrypted with a key that has the hostname and processor family information appended to the end. Software 'Elise': Elise encrypts several of its files, including configuration files. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to XOR encrypt files to be sent to C2. Software 'EnvyScout': EnvyScout can Base64 encode payloads. Software 'SplatDropper': SplatDropper has also utilized XOR encrypted payload. Software 'Rising Sun': Configuration data used by Rising Sun has been encrypted using an RC4 stream algorithm. Software 'HeartCrypt': HeartCrypt strings are encrypted via a single-byte XOR operation rotating over a hard-coded key, possibly provided by the PaaS customers. Software 'LunarMail': LunarMail has used RC4 and AES to encrypt strings and its exfiltration configuration respectively. Software 'ANELLDR': ANELLDR can update its encryption key to AES-256-CBC and re-encrypt its payload, overwriting the original payload file with the newly encrypted data. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu uses a custom algorithm to obfuscate its internal strings and uses hardcoded keys. Mispadu also uses encoded configuration files and has encoded payloads using Base64. Software 'Dacls': Dacls can encrypt its configuration file with AES CBC. Software 'njRAT': njRAT has included a base64 encoded executable. Software 'Kerrdown': Kerrdown can encrypt, encode, and compress multiple layers of shellcode. Software 'BeaverTail': BeaverTail has obfuscated strings of code with Base64 encoding within the JavaScript version of the malware. BeaverTail has also utilized the open-source tool JavaScript-Obfuscator to obfuscate strings and functions. Software 'CorKLOG': CorKLOG has encrypted collected contents using RC4. CorKLOG has also utilized XOR encrypted strings. Software 'IPsec Helper': IPsec Helper contains an embedded XML configuration file with an encrypted list of command and control servers. These are written to an external configuration file during execution. Software 'StealBit': StealBit stores obfuscated DLL file names in its executable. Software 'DUSTPAN': DUSTPAN decrypts an embedded payload. Software 'HexEval Loader': HexEval Loader has encoded module names and C2 URLs as hexadecimal strings in attempts to evade analysis. Software 'PS1': PS1 is distributed as a set of encrypted files and scripts. Software 'KeyBoy': In one version of KeyBoy, string obfuscation routines were used to hide many of the critical values referenced in the malware. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay has downloaded as a XOR-encrypted payload. Software 'Waterbear': Waterbear has used RC4 encrypted shellcode and encrypted functions. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has encrypted and encoded configuration data with Base64 and XOR functions. Software 'Winnti for Linux': Winnti for Linux can encode its configuration file with single-byte XOR encoding. Software 'Seasalt': Seasalt obfuscates configuration data. Software 'StoneDrill': StoneDrill has obfuscated its module with an alphabet-based table or XOR encryption. Software 'IcedID': IcedID has utilzed encrypted binaries and base64 encoded strings. Software 'Tsundere Botnet': Tsundere Botnet’s loader contained AES-CBC/PKCS7 encrypted blobs, which were descrypted and written to disk. Software 'DEADWOOD': DEADWOOD contains an embedded, AES-encrypted resource named METADATA that contains configuration information for follow-on execution. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can encrypt and encode its configuration file. Software 'Chrommme': Chrommme can encrypt sections of its code to evade detection. Software 'LunarWeb': The LunarWeb install files have been encrypted with AES-256. Software 'Attor': Strings in Attor's components are encrypted with a XOR cipher, using a hardcoded key and the configuration data, log files and plugins are encrypted using a hybrid encryption scheme of Blowfish-OFB combined with RSA. Software 'Caminho': Caminho can use code flattening for payload obfuscation. Software 'REvil': REvil has used encrypted strings and configuration files. Software 'HOMEFRY': Some strings in HOMEFRY are obfuscated with XOR x56. Software 'SPAWNCHIMERA': SPAWNCHIMERA has encoded a private key with XOR. SPAWNCHIMERA has also encrypted data to be extracted using AES encryption. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has encrypted both MDeployer and MS4 Killer payloads with RC4. Software 'Smoke Loader': Smoke Loader uses a simple one-byte XOR method to obfuscate values in the malware. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has used an encrypted configuration file for its loader. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito’s installer is obfuscated with a custom crypter to obfuscate the installer. Software 'CozyCar': The payload of CozyCar is encrypted with simple XOR with a rotating key. Software 'ThreatNeedle': ThreatNeedle has been compressed and obfuscated using RC4, AES, or XOR. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware is distributed as an encrypted payload. Software 'PyDCrypt': PyDCrypt has been compiled and encrypted with PyInstaller, specifically using the --key flag during the build phase. Software 'VERMIN': VERMIN is obfuscated using the obfuscation tool called ConfuserEx. Software 'Micropsia': Micropsia obfuscates the configuration with a custom Base64 and XOR. Software 'Kevin': Kevin has Base64-encoded its configuration file. Software 'ROAMINGHOUSE': ROAMINGHOUSE can embed a ZIP file containing UPPERCUT components into three base64 encoded parts. Software 'Chinoxy': Chinoxy has encrypted its configuration file. Software 'DOGCALL': DOGCALL is encrypted using single-byte XOR. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor can encrypt its payload. Software 'Lumma Stealer': Lumma Stealer has used AES-encrypted payloads contained within PowerShell scripts. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY has used encrypted strings in its installer. Software 'SUPERNOVA': SUPERNOVA contained Base64-encoded strings. Software 'Carberp': Carberp has used XOR-based encryption to mask C2 server locations within the trojan. Software 'Emotet': Emotet uses obfuscated URLs to download a ZIP file. Software 'SpeakUp': SpeakUp encodes its second-stage payload with Base64. Software 'XORIndex Loader': XORIndex Loader has encoded module names and C2 URLs as hexadecimal strings in attempts to evade analysis. Software 'PHPsert': PHPsert can use multiple obfuscation techniques including XOR encoding, hexadecimal character representation, string concatenation, and randomized variable names. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda has been obfuscated and contains encrypted functions. Software 'Hi-Zor': Hi-Zor uses various XOR techniques to obfuscate its components. Software 'HiddenWasp': HiddenWasp encrypts its configuration and payload. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus has used a pseudo random number generator (PRNG) algorithm and a rolling XOR key to obfuscate strings. Software 'GoldenSpy': GoldenSpy's uninstaller has base64-encoded its variables. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor can use encrypted string blocks for obfuscation. Software 'WastedLocker': The WastedLocker payload includes encrypted strings stored within the .bss section of the binary file. Software 'Winnti for Windows': Winnti for Windows has the ability to encrypt and compress its payload. Software 'XCSSET': Older XCSSET variants use `xxd` to encode modules. Later versions pass an `xxd` or `base64` encoded blob through multiple decoding stages to reconstruct the module name, AppleScript, or shell command. For example, the initial network request uses three layers of hex decoding before executing a curl command in a shell. Software 'Exaramel for Linux': Exaramel for Linux uses RC4 for encrypting the configuration. Software 'HermeticWizard': HermeticWizard has the ability to encrypt PE files with a reverse XOR loop. Software 'Gazer': Gazer logs its actions into files that are encrypted with 3DES. It also uses RSA to encrypt resources. Software 'Hildegard': Hildegard has encrypted an ELF file. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has obfuscated code using RC4, XOR, and RSA. Group 'Transparent Tribe': Transparent Tribe has dropped encoded executables on compromised hosts. Group 'APT18': APT18 obfuscates strings in the payload. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan has obfuscated code using base64. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used base64 encoding and ECDH-P256 encryption for payloads. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used malware to drop encrypted CAB files. Group 'Saint Bear': Saint Bear initial payloads included encoded follow-on payloads located in the resources file of the first-stage loader. Group 'APT33': APT33 has used base64 to encode payloads. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has obfuscated code within files by converting hexadecimal strings to decimal numbers using the `CLng function` in combination with processing arithmetic operations and leveraging the `Chr function` to generate readable characters. Kimsuky has also encoded files with Base64 and RC4. Kimsuky has utilized XOR and RC4 to encode malicious payloads. Group 'BITTER': BITTER has used a RAR SFX dropper to deliver malware.
